message.content: Adversaries may exploit kernel modules and extensions to achieve persistence and privilege escalation by modifying the kernel to automatically execute programs on system boot. Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs) in Linux and kernel extensions (kexts) in macOS extend kernel functionality without requiring a system reboot. Malicious LKMs can act as kernel-mode rootkits, providing capabilities such as hiding files, processes, and network activity, tampering with logs, and enabling unauthorized access. Kexts in macOS, although deprecated in favor of System Extensions since macOS Catalina 10.15, can still be used as "Legacy System Extensions" and require a developer ID for signing. Adversaries can leverage these mechanisms to maintain persistence and escalate privileges, as seen in malware like Drovorub and Skidmap, and during operations like CuckooBees. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strict code signing policies, enabling System Integrity Protection (SIP) on macOS, monitoring for unauthorized kernel module loading, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious kernel-level activities.
